body_txt: The placebo effect refers to the fact that patients typically experience some improvement in their symptoms after receiving treatment ... even when the 'treatment' is something completely useless for their condition (stereotypically, sugar pills). The most common use is in drug trials, in which a control group is given a placebo, to compare the effects in case the drug actually is only effective due to the placebo effect, or is even worse. The opposite is the nocebo effect , where patients report side effects, despite not having received anything that could genuinely cause those side effects. For more information, as well as modern medicine's best guesses as to why exactly this happens, see The Other Wiki's article. It's a very complicated combination many different difficulties in medical research, but the one thing it's not is a genuine effect. See Magic Feather in terms of the plot. A more realistic version of Clap Your Hands If You Believe . May figure into aversions of No Control Group . Related to Brand Names Are Better . If the person prescribing the placebo knows it's fake, it may be a Motivational Lie . Compare All-Natural Snake Oil and Spice Rack Panacea for "natural" remedies that often prove to be placebos. It can be considered a form of Self-Fulfilling Prophecy . When this works on Applied Phlebotinum , it's (naturally enough) the Placebotinum Effect . Compare Fake High for when this happens with intoxicants.
